coredata.dc:description: Magneto-photoluminescent nanomaterials have emerged as highly appealing systems. However, achieving colloidal stability is challenging, as most strategies produce rather large particles. Therefore, aqueous dispersions are generally prone to sedimentation or phase separations induced by an external magnetic field. We propose a novel approach involving the colloidal mixture of carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), achieved by adjusting the physicochemical parameters of the dispersions individually to produce a stable fluorescent magnetic liquid (FML). Long-term colloidal stability (even under an external magnetic field) was achieved in water by fine-tuning pH, ionic strength, and surface functionalization of magnetic fluids based on core@shell nanoparticles and fluorescent dispersions of nitrogen-rich carbon nanodots. The pH-dependent electrostatic interparticle interactions were elucidated through zetametry. A multiscale analysis based on optical microscopy and small-angle X-ray scattering confirmed the colloidal stability of the FML. These results demonstrated low interparticle interactions and the absence of irreversible aggregation. The magnetic properties were studied by SQUID magnetometry. The saturation magnetization and dipolar interactions between MNPs remained practically unaffected by the presence of CDs. On the other hand, fluorescence spectroscopy and optical absorption data showed a considerable quenching effect in the CDs emission intensity due to MNPs. Nevertheless, the fluorescence with nanosecond decay lifetimes, tunability, and emission spectrum were maintained. Additionally, we assessed the capability of the FML for magnetohyperthermia. The heat generation efficiency was preserved, showing possible use in such therapeutic applications. Further, the proposed approach can be extended to other types of nanomaterials, enabling synergistic effects and enhancing the overall performance of the resulting liquid for targeted technological applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zeta potential as a function of pH for bare magnetic nanoparticles, citrate-coated magnetic nanoparticles, and N-CDs. The yellow rectangle indicates optimal stability pH and ζ zone.

Left: Schematic diagram of the compatibilization process of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and carbon nanodots (CDs) for obtaining a stable fluorescent magnetic fluid (FML). Right: Photos exhibiting a comparison between a pure MNP dispersion (Right tube: precursor ferrofluid without the addition of N-CDs, hence, it does not fluoresce) and the colloidal dispersion of MNPs and N-CDS (left tube: the fluorescent magnetic liquid exhibits a green emission). The transparent portion is just air which occupies the remaining volume of the tube. A magnet is positioned at the top of the image. The colloidal dispersions are suspended inside the tubes against gravity, demonstrating their magnetic character. In both conditions, the dark fluids (under white light) are homogeneous and no phase separation is observed, however, only the FML (MNPs+CDs) shows a green light emission under UV light excitation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magneto-photoluminescent nanomaterials have emerged as highly appealing systems. However, achieving colloidal stability is challenging, as most strategies produce rather large particles. Therefore, aqueous dispersions are generally prone to sedimentation or phase separations induced by an external magnetic field. We propose a novel approach involving the colloidal mixture of carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), achieved by adjusting the physicochemical parameters of the dispersions individually to produce a stable fluorescent magnetic liquid (FML). Long-term colloidal stability (even under an external magnetic field) was achieved in water by fine-tuning pH, ionic strength, and surface functionalization of magnetic fluids based on core@shell nanoparticles and fluorescent dispersions of nitrogen-rich carbon nanodots. The pH-dependent electrostatic interparticle interactions were elucidated through zetametry. A multiscale analysis based on optical microscopy and small-angle X-ray scattering confirmed the colloidal stability of the FML. These results demonstrated low interparticle interactions and the absence of irreversible aggregation. The magnetic properties were studied by SQUID magnetometry. The saturation magnetization and dipolar interactions between MNPs remained practically unaffected by the presence of CDs. On the other hand, fluorescence spectroscopy and optical absorption data showed a considerable quenching effect in the CDs emission intensity due to MNPs. Nevertheless, the fluorescence with nanosecond decay lifetimes, tunability, and emission spectrum were maintained. Additionally, we assessed the capability of the FML for magnetohyperthermia. The heat generation efficiency was preserved, showing possible use in such therapeutic applications. Further, the proposed approach can be extended to other types of nanomaterials, enabling synergistic effects and enhancing the overall performance of the resulting liquid for targeted technological applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic nanoparticles and fluorescent nanoparticles are normally regarded as two separate classes of nano-objects presenting very distinct and unique features: the magnetic and fluorescent properties, respectively. Nevertheless, they are often employed in the same application fields due to a handful of shared similarities, such as biocompatibility, sensing capabilities, and catalytic properties. The synthesis of mixed magneto-photoluminescent systems has been gathering the attention of several scientific groups, intending to unify these very compelling characteristics in a single adaptive and responsive smart material. Indeed, the effective combination of the two properties may contribute to the emergence of enhanced applications such as associated targeting, labeling and sorting [1,2], simultaneous bioimaging and therapy [3] and multimodal contrast agents (fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging) [4,5].

The benefits of multifunctional nanocomposites usually come along with an increase in the complexity of the system and a significant restriction of the conditions in which the material preserves its desirable properties. In the literature, different mechanisms have been proposed in order to combine magnetic and photoluminescent components, such as coating by chemisorption [6], encapsulating various nano-objects by a polymer shell [7,8] or confining one type of nanoparticle inside another [9]. The resulting layering increases nanostructure size, thereby preventing long-term colloidal stability with or without the application of an external magnetic field. These matters may or may not pose a problem depending on the desired application. For instance, magneto-induced demixing is useful in achieving highly effective magnetic separation when considering nanoadsorbent applications [10,11]. Contrarily, it is undoubtedly an important factor for further biomedical applications to preserve colloidal stability and prevent particle coagulation [12]. Thus, different regimes of particle–particle and/or particle-solvent interactions enable the exploitation of a wider variety of nanoscale ordering [13–15].

In the present paper, we develop a new class of aqueous fluorescent magnetic liquids (FMLs) by the colloidal mixture of carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), which remain stable even under an external magnetic field. These materials have been initially designed as nanoscale probes for fracture inspection of metallic materials [16]. Here, we demonstrate that they are also good candidates for theranostic applications as multimodal contrast agents and as magnetic nanoheaters.

More precisely, we combine nitrogen-rich carbon nanoparticles (N-CDs) emitting in the green spectral range [17] and core@shell-based magnetic nanoparticles (CoFe
2
O
4
@ γ -Fe
2
O
3
) to produce a stable fluorescent magnetic nanofluid. Controlling the pH and ionic strength of the medium, we exploited the electrical double-layer properties of MNPs and N-CDs to propose a promising route to disperse the nano-objects avoiding aggregation or sedimentation. After successful colloidal stabilization, we thoroughly characterized the nanofluids, highlighting the preservation of magnetic characteristics of MNPs and the N-CDs emitting properties.

The proposed method carries the advantage of the possibility of tuning the individual properties of either type of nanoparticle independently (through each synthesis process), while vastly preserving them in the nanofluid at neutral pH medium and maintaining the long-term colloidal stability. The whole of the obtained results demonstrate that the nanofluids prepared by this colloidal route have a strong potential in a wide range of sensing applications from biomedicine to industry.

Colloidal chemistry shows great potential for synthesizing and/or dispersing various nanostructures [18–22], and most of the techniques rely on the characteristics of a stable colloid. The stability of a colloidal system depends on the complex interplay between attractive and repulsive energies. The proposed approach to produce our fluorescent magnetic nanofluid relies on electrostatic repulsion that prevents the particles from coming into close contact and aggregating. In this context, the elaboration process of the fluorescent magnetic fluids consists of three independent steps: (I) Production of fluorescent aqueous colloids based on N-CDs; (II) Synthesis of electrostatically stabilized aqueous magnetic fluids at neutral pH; and (III) Colloidal mixing by tuning the physicochemical parameters of each dispersion. All reagents used in this study were of analytical grade and were utilized without further purification. Type-1 ultrapure water was employed in all experiments.

Carbon dots are a relatively recent but now well-established family of carbon-based nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm, displaying bright fluorescence, which can be tuned across the visible range. Besides the intense light emission, they are characterized by ease of synthesis and functionalization, photostability, and biocompatibility, making them very appealing for multiple applications [23–26].

The synthesis of N-CDs is achieved by the microwave-induced thermal decomposition of organic precursors, consolidated in the literature [17,27–30] as a direct, simple and low-cost method constituted of a single step with ordinary reagents and equipment. In short, β -C
3
N
4
green-emitting N-CDs of typical 5 nm diameter are synthesized by microwave-induced or thermally-induced decomposition of citric acid (3.0 g) and urea (3.0 g) in type 1 water (10.0 mL). The carbonization process requires only a few minutes and results in the production of a black powder. The dark material is strongly hygroscopic, so after being brought back to room temperature, it is dried in vacuum for 90 min. The final product is a fine dry powder consisting of aggregates of N-CDs that are ready to be dispersed in water thanks to their hydrophilic surface. This behavior is linked to the development of a negative surface charge attributed to carboxylate and oxidized nitrogen surface groups [31,32].

Ferrite-based materials have been extensively utilized in several applications, including technological [33], environmental [11] and biomedical ones [34–37], due to their exceptional properties. Here, they are applied to obtain stable magnetic colloids, or ferrofluids. The synthesis of the employed ferrofluid (FFs) follows a solidly established experimental protocol comprehensively described in previous works [38,39]. Ferrite MNPs with typical 7 nm size are firstly produced by a modified coprecipitation method, which creates a core@shell chemical structure (CoFe
2
O
4
@ γ -Fe
2
O
3
). This particular composition and architecture endow the nanoparticles with a strongly magnetic core and a highly chemically stable and tunable shell [18,37,40,41]. Then, the nanoparticles are electrostatically stabilized in an acidic medium thanks to the repulsion between their positively charged surface. Afterward, we coat the surface of the MNPs with citrate ligands as detailed elsewhere [42]. Once the surface functionalization is complete, the citrate-coated nanoparticles are magnetically separated from the aqueous phase. We then carry out a thorough washing process using a standard citric acid solution [14] to achieve a free citrate concentration of 10−2 mol/L. Subsequently, to ensure stable ferrofluids under neutral conditions, the pH of the medium is adjusted to 7.0 with a 1 mol/L NaOH solution. These steps ensure complete coverage of the nanoparticles with citrate and give them a negative charge in neutral media [43].

The strategy for achieving colloidal compatibility between the two types of dispersions is to adjust the physicochemical parameters of the N-CDs medium to match those of the ferrofluids. For this purpose, we add citric acid to the N-CDs colloidal dispersion to keep the free citrate concentration at 10−2 mol/L, while setting the pH to 7.0 with 1.0 mol/L NaOH solution.

From this point on, the combining process is straightforward. One can directly mix both dispersions as the proposed method adjusts physicochemical parameters such as ionic strength, nature of the counter-ions, pH, and particle concentrations before adding one fluid to the other. Here, the free citrate ions and pH are set on each colloidal dispersion to match similar values: [citrate
free
]
= 1
0
− 2
mol/L (adjusted with citric acid) and pH
= 7 . 0
(adjusted with NaOH). Therefore, these values are preserved before, during, and after the mixing procedure. On the other hand, the concentrations of magnetic and fluorescent nanoparticles will be reduced by half as equal volumes of each colloidal dispersion are combined. Hence, the precursor colloids should have twice the intended concentration of the final FML.

The zeta potential ( ζ ) of the MNPs (bare and citrated ones) and N-CDs was determined by Electrophoretic Light Scattering (ELS) experiments using a ZetaSizer (model NanoZS 90, Malvern Panalytical) with a disposable folded capillary cell (DTS 1070). The samples were diluted to a volume fraction of 0.01% at different pH conditions, in the range of 2–12, using NaNO
3
0.01 mol/L as background electrolyte. The pH of the medium was adjusted using 0.01 mol/L NaOH and 0.01 mol/L HNO
3
solutions. All measurements were carried out in triplicate at a constant temperature of 25 °C.

In typical ELS experiments, the electrophoretic mobility of the nanoparticles ( μ ) is obtained from particle velocity measurements through Laser Doppler Velocimetry using a 4.0 mW He-Ne laser of 633 nm wavelength at a scattering angle of 17°. Then, the μ values are converted into zeta potentials using Henry’s equation [44]

In order to assess and verify the colloidal stability of our nanofluids, we performed Small Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS) experiments, carried out in a Xeuss 2.0 (Xenocs) instrument, using a Cu K
α

During the experiment, samples are conditioned within sealed polyimide tubes (Xeuss) or mica windows (LNLS).

In particular, as carbonaceous NPs exhibit a significantly lower contrast than iron oxide NPs, it results in a 3-order-of-magnitude difference in absolute units (cm−1) in these concentrations. Fig. 3 confirms that the N-CDs signal would account marginally in the total scattered intensity by the mixture.

In this context, these approximations presumably allow us to extract insightful information about the nanofluid in the 1–100 nm range by the development of I(Q) as a function of w , while ϕ is kept at a constant 1.0 vol%. However, note that while it is feasible to gather information regarding the interparticle interactions and/or aggregate formation of MNPs, the organization of N-CDs remains unresolved by this approach.

The nanofluids had their magnetic characterization carried out in a Cryogenic S700X-R SQUID Magnetometer. For the measurement, the samples were conditioned in Plexiglas® sample holders sealed off with tetrahydrofuran (THF). Magnetization versus applied field was recorded both at room temperature (300 K) and at low temperature (5 K). Room temperature measurements allow us to assess the magnetization in typical application temperatures. Conversely, at 5 K, magnetic anisotropy and the effect of interparticle interactions are unraveled.

The low-temperature coercivity is used to characterize the anisotropy, while the characterization of the interaction state of the colloids is done by applying the
Δ M
Thamm–Hesse formalism [47]. In this approach, the experimental deviation from the expected on a noninteractive system by the Stoner–Wohlfarth model [48] is used as a means to gauge the global interaction regime in the sample. In a non-interactive regime, the remanence relations state that the initial magnetization curve at a given H value is expected to be the average between the upper and lower branches of the hysteresis loops. In this manner, the deviation

The magnetic nanoheating capabilities of the studied samples are investigated by magnetohyperthermia essays carried out in a nanoScale Biomagnetics D5 System. In an experiment, the sample is subjected to an AC magnetic field while the temperature increase is recorded.

The fluorescence of the FML samples was characterized using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy. The emission spectra were recorded with a JASCO FP-6500 spectrofluorometer in a 1 cm cuvette. The time-resolved fluorescence measurements were carried out by a tunable laser system consisting of an optical parametric oscillator pumped by a Q-switched Nd:YAg laser (5 ns pulses at 10 Hz repetition rate). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an intensified charge-coupled device (CCD) camera, integrating the signal within temporal windows of 0.5 ns duration after variable delays from the laser pulse.

The emission of the visible band was recorded from samples prepared with two N-CDs concentrations of 1.0 g/L and 10.0 g/L and in various concentrations of MNPs between 0.1 and 1.0 vol%. Moreover, it was checked that the magnetic nanoparticles with the CoFe

iron oxide structure do not significantly emit in this spectral range.

The pH dependence of the zeta potential of MNPs and N-CDs is shown in Fig. 1. It provides useful insights into the proposed protocol to colloidally stabilize an aqueous FML. The observed zeta potential profiles reflect the mechanism of charge regulation of each type of nanoparticle, according to the chemical nature of its surface groups. In the case of the bare MNPs, the surface charge arises from the protonation (deprotonation) equilibrium of the metal-hydroxyl groups MOH, which leads to MOH
2
+
(MO
−
) depending on the pH of the medium (M is the metallic ion on nanoparticle surface) [49,50]. For the citrate-coated MNPs and the N-CDs, the charge is mainly due to the carboxyl groups present at the surface, which are progressively deprotonated as the pH shifts towards alkaline values.

At low pH conditions, N-CDs and MNPs present surface charges of opposite signs, which means that the mixture in acidic media is likely to cause aggregation effects due to strong attractive interactions between the two different types of nanoparticles. This would induce particle sedimentation over time, therefore no colloidal stability. In a strongly alkaline medium, N-CDs and MNPs hold a negative surface charge, potentially allowing colloidal stability thanks to electrostatic repulsion between particles. Nevertheless, highly alkaline conditions limit the applications of the final colloid, especially in a biological medium, and also might undergo irreversible modifications on N-CDs emitting spectrum. Moreover, high-pH media can be very corrosive, which must be avoided in industrial settings.

A similar repulsive interparticle regime can be achieved at pH 7.0 in the case of N-CDs and citrate-coated MNPs. The advantage of working at this pH is preserving the N-CDs emission spectrum, as described later in the text, together with the benefits of using a physiological pH. Thus, based on the zeta potential profiles, Fig. 1, one can realize that the region highlighted in yellow between pH 6.0 and 8.0, where both N-CDs and citrate-coated MNPs exhibit moderate to high negative zeta potential values, is the most likely to achieve a stable FML.

On these grounds, we propose a route to obtain a hybrid nanofluid at neutral pH, whose steps are depicted in Fig. 2 and were briefly described in the experimental section. After obtaining the N-CDs and citrate-coated MNPs dispersions at pH 7.0 (Steps 1 and 2), the mixing procedure (Step 3) is performed by finely tuning the pH and ionic strength of the N-CDs dispersion medium using NaOH and citric acid. To avoid the desorption of citrate ligands from the MNPs in the final mixture, we keep the concentration of free citrate ions in the N-CDs dispersion at 10−2 mol/L to account for the dilution effect. Then, the FML is obtained by adding, under stirring, this N-CDs dispersion to the citrate-coated MNPs one.

In Fig. 2, photos of two samples with and without N-CDs are shown. The samples do not present significant differences when exposed to visible light, but, under UV light, the fluorescence produced by the N-CDs can be perceived. The sample of just MNPs does not fluoresce, as expected. The fact that the samples are being held by a magnet against gravity shows that no induced magnetic phase separation occurs. Also, the manipulation of the fluid via magnetic forces is possible in both cases and the fluorescence is noticeable, even in the presence of an external magnetic field. Furthermore, we observe that the FML remains macroscopically stable on a time scale of several years. This has been verified in a variety of concentration combinations for MNPs and N-CDs. For the magnetic component, the volume fraction ϕ of magnetic nanoparticles was varied between 0.1 and 1.0% (5.2 and 52 mg/ml ), while for the fluorescent component, the N-CDs concentration w was varied from 0.5 to 15.0 g/L.

Moreover, the stability at the nanoscale can be also confirmed by SAXS results. From the data shown in Fig. 3a, the mixture of N-CDs with the citrated ferrofluid (blue dashed curve) does not show critical changes if compared with the pure ferrofluid (red curve). The experimental structure factors shown in Fig. 3b for various N-CDs concentrations were calculated by dividing the scattering intensity of the FML by the intensity of the pure ferrofluid.

S
( Q )
is close to 1 for
Q > 0 . 2
nm
− 1
, but progressively increases with the concentration of N-CDs in smaller Q -values. Since
S
( Q )
> 1
, this effect indicates that the introduction of N-CDs reduces the overall interparticle repulsion of MNPs. The observed increment at
Q < 0 . 2
nm
− 1
is almost negligible at
w = 5 . 0
g/L. However, it is more prominent at 15 g/L (
S
( Q → 0 )
< 1 . 2
in rough extrapolation of the data). This concentration-dependent effect could be a consequence of: the contribution of the scattered signal of N-CDs themselves; an increase in ionic strength originating from the N-CDs dispersions which contributes to the screening of the MNPs electrostatic potential; and/or weak interactions between the MNPs and N-CDs.

In the least attractive case, as detailed in Fig. 3a, the scattered intensity from the N-CDs could only introduce up to a 0.1% intensity increase at 5.0 g/L. Thus, it seems unlikely to be the main cause of the 2% change in
S
( Q )
shown in Fig. 3b.

It is known that some side products are present in the N-CDs samples produced by this route [32], which may affect the MNPs electric double layer by shielding the electrostatic potential. Lastly, the interparticle interaction between N-CDs and MNPs is expected to be globally repulsive as both NPs are negatively charged. However, some particles may collide and combine momentarily (or permanently) to form small aggregates. In these two scenarios, the increase in N-CD concentration would reduce the global stability by either increasing the ionic strength or promoting the formation of large particle structures. With this in view, the structure factor development indicating less repulsive interparticle interactions as a function of N-CD concentration corroborates both these assumptions.

Anyhow, in Fig. 2 one observes that no phase separation occurs in the FML suspended by the magnet. In addition, the optical microscopy images in the bottom panel of Fig. 3 exhibit almost no difference between the FF and the FML besides perhaps a small change in color/absorption. Therefore, the stability is still assured even in the presence of an external magnetic field and no demixing was observed even at micrometer scales, despite a slight reduction in the overall MNP interparticle repulsion.

The magnetic properties of the ferrofluid were practically unaltered by the introduction of N-CDs. Fig. 4 presents the experimental results comparing the magnetic properties of the pure ferrofluid (
ϕ = 1 . 0
vol%) (red line) and the FML obtained by mixing the FF at
ϕ = 1 . 0
vol% with N-CDs at
w = 10 . 0
g/L (blue circles).

The top main graph in Fig. 4 shows the room temperature magnetization curve ( T = 300 K) which allows us to assess
M
( H )
in typical application temperatures. This curve highly depends on the size distribution of the sample and follows the Langevin formalism, for noninteracting nanoparticles. Therefore, the superposition of the data indicates that the addition of the N-CDs does not induce any kind of sedimentation process, as the size distribution remains unaffected. In addition, by observing the inset in Fig. 4a, where a plot of
M
( 1 /
μ
0
H )
is shown, one can see that the saturation magnetization, obtained as
1 / H ⟶ 0
, does not change significantly, showing a difference smaller than 1.5% when the N-CDs are present. This assures that the magnetic properties of the FMLs are maintained at room temperature and that the introduction of N-CDs in the ferrofluid dispersions does not induce any demagnetizing effect.

The dipolar interactions among nanoparticles affect some of the magnetic properties, which can then be used to probe the state of interparticle magnetic interactions. Several experimental techniques are used with this purpose in the literature [51–55]. Here, to characterize the interaction state of the colloids, Fig. 4b presents the magnetization deviation
Δ M
obtained by using the Thamm–Hesse formalism, as described in Eq. (7). The data shows that
Δ M
is primarily negative, revealing that demagnetizing interactions (dipolar) are predominant. The FML presents equal behavior, indicating that the presence of the N-CDs does not change the dipolar interparticle interactions throughout the sample, despite the minor changes in the global balance of interactions indicated by the SAXS measurements.

The inset graph in Fig. 4b shows the
M
( H )
curves at 5 K, where the open loop and the presence of the nonzero coercive field is related to the magnetic anisotropy of the MNPs. Once again, the curves are superposed indicating no changes in magnetic anisotropy and coercivity are added by introducing the N-CDs.

Furthering, the magnetic nanoheating of the FML is investigated as a function of applied field and frequency. As expected, the top graph in Fig. 5 confirms that the N-CDs dispersion alone does not show any temperature increase in the presence of the AC magnetic field. On the other hand, both MNP-based samples present a significant temperature increase, while the FML shows just a slight delay in the heating process when compared to the pure FF. Indeed, no significant difference in the
S P A
( H )
values can be observed in Fig. 5 bottom graph, regardless of the frequency.

These results attest that the FML comprehensively retains the magnetic characteristics of the precursor ferrofluid, independently of the temperature. Moreover, the conversion of electromagnetic energy into heat is also preserved as the SPA remains practically unchanged after the addition of the N-CDs.

To evaluate the degree of the emission reduction, Fig. 6a exhibits a comparison between an aqueous solution of
w = 1 . 0
g/L of bare N-CDs with the FML containing
w = 1 . 0
g/L of N-CDs and
ϕ = 0 . 1 %
of MNPs. The emission band intensity decreases about 8 times in the presence of just 0.1 vol% of MNPs. Increasing the number of MNPs produces a further increase in emission quenching (Fig. 6b). Despite the quenching, the typical tunability – an important property for the potential applications of these FMLs – of N-CDs is mostly preserved as shown in Fig. 6c.

In principle, three phenomena can cause a quenching of N-CD emission in the presence of the MNPs. (i) Excitation light could be partially absorbed by MNPs, such that less of it can successfully excite the N-CDs. (ii) Part of the emitted light from N-CDs is reabsorbed by MNPs. (iii) Direct short-range electronic interaction between N-CDs and MNPs, that is, either electron transfer or energy transfer phenomena, leads to the quenching of the emissive state of N-CDs.

Among these three phenomena, only the last one represents a direct interaction between the two types of nanoparticles, therefore it is interesting to isolate this effect out of the total observed quenching. In this respect, the extent to which the first two phenomena affect the intensity of N-CD emission can be estimated based on the absorption spectra of the two types of nanoparticles. This is because the absorption coefficient of MNPs at the excitation (emission) wavelengths, respectively, is the crucial factor in determining how much of the excitation (emitted) light can be absorbed (reabsorbed) by the MNPs.

Once the absorption spectrum of MNPs is considered (not shown), we calculated that the reabsorption effects (point ii in the list above) are negligible, but the absorption of excitation light from MNP (point i) is quite relevant. At the lowest MNP concentration, we experimentally observe an overall quenching by a factor of 8, meaning the intensity is reduced to
1
/
8
= 0.125 concerning the pure N-CD dispersion. However, once we correct the effect of excitation absorption from MNPs, we estimate that the actual quenching from MNP-N-CD interactions (point iii) is only 0.40.

Anyway, despite the significant quenching induced by the MNPs, the sample remains emissive enough that its fluorescence can be easily visible to the naked eye (see the photograph in Fig. 6). Combined with the magnetic response, the fluorescence properties make the FML potentially useful for several important applications, such as bioimaging or fluorescent sensing.

The emission lifetime of the bare N-CDs results in 5.7 ns, inside the typical range of this type of sample [29,56]. The lifetime of the FML results shortened to 4.2 ns. The decrease in the emission lifetime indicates that part of the quenching is a so-called dynamic quenching, caused by the collisions between N-CDs and MNPs particles. Despite this, the ratio between the lifetime values (4.2 ns / 5.7 ns = 0.74) does not account for the total emission quenching observed in the steady-state experiments (0.18 to 0.4, as mentioned before), suggesting that part of the quenching is dynamic and part of the quenching is static. The latter is probably due, despite the electrostatic repulsion, to the formation of a small fraction of non-fluorescent complexes between the two species which is consistent with the small attractive interaction observed in SAXS measurements.

Even neglecting the possibility of complexation, one must remember that the concentrations at play in the FML are indeed extremely high, to the extent that a certain degree of interaction is unavoidable. Indeed, considering the portion of space occupied by N-CDs or MNPs volume and their concentration in solution, it is possible to estimate the average distance between the two types of nanoparticles, which turns out to be as low as 14 nm when N-CDs and MNP concentrations are 1 g/L and 1%, respectively. With these numbers, it becomes statistically likely that a sizeable fraction of photoexcited N-CDs will fall within a few nanometers distance from the closest MNPs. In this distance range, an energy transfer from the photoexcited N-CDs to the MNPs appears to be the most likely photophysical interaction mechanism.

The mentioned quenching hardly changes the emission bandshape of N-CDs, and does not hinder the tunability of the N-CDs emission, which is preserved within a wide spectral range. This suggests that the interactions between the two types of NPs remain very weak except during their association into composites and/or collisions.

The magnetic properties of the FMLs can be tuned solely by the MNPs concentration, as the N-CDs have negligible impact on them. Indeed, as both SAXS and photoluminescence indicate, the formation of complexes is very limited, explaining why we do not detect any significant changes in the global regime of magnetic interactions. This contrasts with what is observed when the carbon nanomaterials are in direct contact with the MNPs surface [57,58]. In the FMLs, since electrostatic repulsion between the two types of nanomaterials avoids the formation of complexes, the addition of N-CDs does not actively influence the magnetization and anisotropy of the original MNPs, thus also preserving the original nanoheating capacity.

A colloidal approach was successfully employed to obtain stable fluorescent magnetic liquids by combining bare nitrogen-rich carbon nanodots with citrate-coated core@shell magnetic nanoparticles in neutral medium. The proposed method relies on particle–particle and particle-solvent interactions to tune nanoscale ordering, in contrast to usual single nanoparticle strategies where the fluorescent and magnetic nanomaterials are combined by chemisorption, encapsulation, or confinement techniques [6–9].

SAXS measurements supported the colloidal stability of the magnetofluorescent nanofluid, even in the presence of an external magnetic field. The sample exhibits a colloidal behavior similar to typical citrated ferrofluids with a slight reduction in the overall repulsion between the magnetic nanoparticles [37,59]. The magnetic characteristics of the fluorescent liquid were also highly preserved. The presence of N-CDs practically did not affect the saturation magnetization, magnetic interaction regimes, and SPA of the original citrated magnetic fluid, demonstrating the potential for magnetohyperthermia.

Fluorescence experiments show decreased emission intensity by adding MNPs if compared to bare N-CDs aqueous dispersions. The reduction is attributed to MNPs absorption of the excitation beam and quenching through surface interactions between N-CDs and MNPs. Based on lifetime measurements, the quenching via interparticle interactions occurs partly through a static mechanism (complex formation) and partly through a dynamic mechanism (collisions). Despite this loss in emission intensity, the multiscale optical and X-ray scattering analysis combined with magnetic experiments suggests that the formation of complexes is limited and does not alter the main characteristics of either component in the FML. Hence, the resulting fluorescent nanofluids retain their main optical properties: fluorescence with nanosecond decay lifetimes, fluorescence tunability, and similar emission spectrum.

Finally, the study suggests that the proposed colloidal approach can be extended to produce other types of multifunctional liquids based on different nanomaterials to exploit distinct technological applications. Carbon nanodots based on O- or metal-doped g-C
3
N
4
[60,61] combined with gold, silver, or other oxide-based nanoparticles [62–64] can be envisaged to achieve synergistic effects and enhance the overall performance of the resulting liquid mainly in catalytic and adsorptive processes.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental small angle X-ray scattering data of the citrated ferrofluid supporting the low interparticle interaction regime and size distribution at 1%vol concentration. Experimental data of the mixture in acidic conditions in which both nanocomponents preserve similar chemical composition and size, however, at pH 2.5 they present opposite charge signals. Colloidal instabilities are observed in the macroscale with or without the presence of an external magnetic field and globally attractive interparticle interactions are confirmed at nanoscale.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.110320.
